data.title: Alphabet: The Social Media Lawsuit Is Not A Long-Term Risk
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Over the last month, as the overall market has sold off as a result of the war in Iran, Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) shares have dropped 21% since their peak in February. Technically, this would mean that shares are in a bear market.

At the heart of Alphabet’s selloff is the recent finding by a jury in Los Angeles which found that the company’s video streaming platform (YouTube) was liable for promoting social media addiction that caused harm on the Plaintiff (K.G.M or Kaley).

While the market is worrying this could set off a cascading event leading to Alphabet being dragged into more lawsuits, I think this lawsuit will end up being a bump in the road in hindsight.

In essence, I believe this lawsuit is a distraction from Alphabet’s real business lines and durable growth. For investors who want to use Warren Buffett's famous line “be greedy when others are fearful” I believe now could be a good time to take advantage of the dip in share price.

With this, I continue to believe shares of the California-based tech giant are still a Strong Buy. This lawsuit appears concerning on the surface. But I believe that buried in the details of this lawsuit the facts show this will have limited long-term effects on the business.

Why I’m Doing Follow-Up Coverage

When I last wrote on Alphabet earlier this year, I was more focused on the tech giants rapid advancements in quantum computing. I continue to believe this is one of the most important projects the company is working on, and I believe this lawsuit is noise.

But, as Warren Buffett’s mentor Benjamin Graham has said “in the short run the market is a voting machine.” In this case, the market is voting that the outcome of this lawsuit in LA is far more pressing to the long term value of Alphabet’s stock price than the huge developments they have made in the quantum sphere.

In a few words, I disagree. With this, I am doing follow-up coverage to show how this lawsuit is not a long-term risk and that the real opportunities for Alphabet’s upside just got better.

LA Lawsuit Is Not A Risk

On Wednesday, a Jury in California awarded the Plaintiff, Kaley, a total of $6 million in damages as a result of a lawsuit against major social media giants YouTube (part of Alphabet) and Facebook’s owner, Meta Platforms (META).

The markets are not selling off due to the total of $6 million in damages. The markets don’t like this outcome because some participants believe this could open the flood gate to more lawsuits/changing regulations that could put these tech giants in a bad position.

More specifically, some experts are stating:

...[This is] the social media industry’s “Big Tobacco” moment, comparing it with the 1990s, when tobacco companies were forced to pay billions of dollars for lying to the public about the safety and potential harms of their products.

Respectfully, I disagree. To understand how big this could be, we have to look at the fact pattern for this lawsuit.

In this case, the Plaintiff sued the tech giants alleging that the platforms caused her to become addicted to them which exasperated her mental health condition. According to reports:

The 20-year-old woman alleged that she suffered from severe body dysmorphia, depression and suicidal thoughts due to her near-constant use of the apps and the constant notifications that made it difficult for her to stop.

While I sympathize with the Plaintiff and admit social media can clearly be addictive, I am concerned that many similar lawsuits that could arise will be able to demonstrate this fact pattern. Simply being addicted to social media will not entitle you to compensation. The fact pattern will likely have to be similar to this where there is objective harm. This is where I believe most copycat lawsuits will fall apart.

Here in the U.S., to be able to be successful in most civil suits, you have to be able to quantify or explicitly qualify damages the defendant caused you to incur. For many cases of social media addiction, this will be difficult.

Overall, I expect this to be either a one-off or a limited slate of lawsuits by users who are addicted. I will talk more about this in the risks section, but Alphabet does have some federal laws that protect them as well.

Valuation

While Alphabet’s shares have come under pressure over the past two months since I last wrote on them, I believe the valuation story has only gotten better.

If we look at the company’s forward EPS estimates, growth still appears to be strong, with the median Wall Street analyst expecting EPS growth to clock in at 7.04% YoY compared to 2025 EPS numbers.

With this, if we look at overall EPS revisions for 2026, this still appears to be net up compared to what estimates were both 1, 3, and 6 months ago. While some investors are taking this jury outcome and using it as a sell the news event, Wall Street is only getting more bullish from here.

On some equities, I believe institutional investors are wrong. For a big tech company (like Alphabet) I think the smart money is right here. I believe the company’s story will get better going forward. I don’t believe this will become a cascading legal issue.

With EPS growth looking stable, the valuation looks really attractive at these prices. While the company’s forward P/E of 24.28 is 83.4% higher than the sector median, the company’s forward revenue growth is over 236% higher than the sector median. In essence, you are paying a 83.4% premium valuation to unlock over 236% top line growth. I think this still makes sense.

With this, I think shares should be trading at a 100% premium to the sector median forward P/E to account for the fact that the company will (most likely) still continue to book strong growth. This lawsuit will not be a long-term catastrophic risk to the company like the market has been implying with the share price moves over the last 8 weeks.

With this, if we saw the premium expand to 100% from 83.4%, this would represent 19.90% upside from here.

Risks

I believe the biggest risk of this lawsuit is not the effective "fine" the lawsuit has assessed against Alphabet. Rather, as I touched on before, I believe the biggest risk is that this could cascade into more lawsuits against the company nationwide. As Reuters reported:

The Los Angeles trial is meant to serve as a bellwether, or test case, for the thousands of similar lawsuits consolidated in California state courts.

To be clear, and as NPR outlined in their reporting on this lawsuit earlier this week, the verdict by the jury in California is "rare." With it being rare, I expect Alphabet (and Meta for their part) to appeal their respective outcomes of this lawsuit.

At the federal level, the ace in the hole for these companies is Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act of 1996 which protects the major social media platforms (including Alphabet’s YouTube) from being held liable for content that was posted by users on their platform. I believe this is the silver lining.

For Alphabet, the story is straightforward: appeal this ruling (they have already indicated they will) and lean on Section 230 to remove liability from their services as a platform. In essence, the heart of this caselaw going forward will likely be whether the platforms themselves are addictive (and therefore cause harm) or if it's the content itself that is posted on the platform that is addictive.

After all, if platforms like YouTube had no user generated content on them, their value to a consumer would be minimal. I believe Alphabet will lean hard on this during the appeal process which will likely be in front of a series of judges, not a jury. Judges often rule differently on appeal than a Jury may have originally. This is because judges here in the U.S. will most often assess a case on appeal on the basis that the law was applied incorrectly. I think Alphabet via YouTube has a solid case here.

Bottom Line

While Alphabet losing this lawsuit and being ordered to pay $1.5 million + 30% of a remaining $3 million joint judgement between them and Meta is clearly concerning, I am not worried about the overall implications to the company in the long run like the market appears to be.

Alphabet’s business is incredibly diversified with roughly 15% of revenues coming from its YouTube division, which is the part of the company that was targeted in this lawsuit.

Even if this lawsuit led to later lawsuits that somehow cut YouTube’s revenue by 33%, the $20 billion in lost topline revenue could likely be replaced by growth from other divisions of the company. Keep in mind that the company grew 15% YoY on over $400 billion in topline revenue. Even growth that is ½ this pace (7.5% YoY topline growth) would more than offset a 33% drop in YouTube’s revenue due to changes in how they can place ads/push engagement time with users going forward.

In essence, Alphabet’s business is incredibly durable. The market (especially in the last month) has been pricing their business like its brittle. I disagree.

With this, and despite the selloff, shares remain a Strong Buy.

data.title: MNTN: Surprise Product-Led Growth Could Unlock Multiple Expansion
data.description:
MNTN (MNTN) is an ad-tech company based in the US that focuses on performance-based connected TV (CTV) advertising. The platform allows brands to run automated advertising campaigns on TV and measure the ad performance. Having covered a few companies in the ad-tech sector, I found the CTV market to be quite attractive due to the overall shift from linear to digital programming, with the competitive landscape typically segmented by the type of clients being served.

For instance, since MNTN offers its solutions to brands or advertisers that demand advertising slots, then its business is categorized as a DSP (Demand Side Platform). In that sense, it competes with the likes of Trade Desk (TTD) or Vibe. On the other hand, there are also companies offering an SSP (Supply Side Platform) solution on the opposite side to media companies owning the advertising slots, which, in this context, is irrelevant to MNTN’s competitive landscape despite being in the same industry.

Since its May 2025 IPO at $16 per share, overall performance has been underwhelming, with the stock currently trading merely at $8.6 per share, losing half of its IPO valuation. The stock is also down almost -30% YTD, suggesting a relatively cautious market sentiment on the stock as of late.

I initiate my coverage with a buy rating. My 1-year price target of $9.52 suggests that MNTN appears undervalued.

Financial Reviews

Overall, it seems that fundamentals are quite decent and improving. In Q4, MNTN continues to realize double-digit growth as it benefits from the ad budgets shift towards the CTV platform. In Q4 2025, MNTN delivered a revenue of $87.1 million, a 36% YoY growth. For FY 2025, MNTN realized almost 29% growth and a FY revenue of $290 million.

The key highlight in Q4, I believe, is in the bottom-line performance for the FY, which sees an improvement across the board. Overall, this seems to be driven by the strong revenue growth and also the company’s ability to manage the expansion of its cost of revenues. In FY 2025, operating income turned positive to $24 million as a result, displaying a significant swing from last year’s loss.

Meanwhile, adjusted EBITDA (aEBITDA) for the FY reached almost $68 million, reflecting a 23% margin, an expansion from 17.2% the prior year.

Liquidity and cash flow generation are quite solid, in my view, as MNTN finished the FY with almost $210 million of cash and equivalents with zero debts, with the company generating over $56 million of operating cash flow (OCF) for the FY, a better figure compared to last year’s and a stronger position compared to its IPO levels.

Catalyst

I think the catalyst will remain moderate to strong into 2026. Supported by the secular industry shift into CTV digital ads, I believe MNTN’s offerings should continue to be in demand. However, it is also important to give credits to how well MNTN may capture such an opportunity. In that sense, I may project decent growth in 2026 from the potential adoption of its newly-released AI-driven platform QuickFrame.

In short, the platform helps simplify video content generation for SMBs, MNTN’s target market, in a way it could reduce production cost for TV advertising. To a greater extent, I view this as a way for MNTN to possibly capture some market share from its competitor TDD, which focuses on serving larger enterprise teams with bigger advertising budgets. With the platform gaining 5,000 users since its beta launch, as commented by an analyst in the earnings call, the growth prospect appears to be quite promising:

I want to ask about more details, Mark, on just QuickFrame AI. With the adoption ramping here, I think I heard 5,000 users. Would love to hear any insights you have on improvements to the business as a result of it in terms of time-to-market perhaps maybe post-sales cycle?

Source: Q4 earnings call.

Another catalyst that is worth mentioning here is the integration with Magnite (MGNI), an ad-tech company I also recently covered. Since MGNI is an SSP, the integration makes a lot of sense, as it should unlock premium live-streaming ad inventories for MNTN’s existing advertiser users, potentially driving higher conversion rates and larger advertisers into the platform. As expected, the management also was equally optimistic on this prospect as per their comment in earnings call:

Yes. Absolutely. I mean, we're very bullish on that partnership. We have a really good relationship with Magnite. And specifically, when we announced it, we were talking about paused ads, but there's also live sports and other very premium content that we've introduced both through our direct partnerships with the media companies, all of whom we have direct partnerships with, but also through Magnite.

Source: Q4 earnings call.

Risk

Overall, I would say that risk remains minimal to moderate for MNTN. While the escalating geopolitical tension may pose a risk, for instance, through rising energy cost concerns that could make SMBs cut advertising budgets, it is probably too early to grade these risk factors as immediate. Nonetheless, the fact that I mentioned them here means that I am not entirely disregarding this risk factor.

On the other hand, the intensifying competition in the CTV space may present a higher risk factor, in my view, especially from the likes of Meta (META) or TTD, both larger players that could at some point enter MNTN’s space with their SMB-focused solutions that could threaten MNTN’s market share.

Valuation/Pricing

My target price for MNTN is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection, based on the consensus analysis:

  1. Bull scenario (50% probability) assumptions - I assume MNTN exceeds the high-end of the consensus estimate, with the QuickFrame adoption and the MGNI partnership contributing significantly to the overperformance. I would expect MNTN to see a re-rating of its P/S to 2.5x, surpassing today’s level as the market assigns a premium valuation to the stock.

  2. Bear scenario (50% probability) assumptions - I expect a relatively muted sentiment in the ad-tech market, due to concerns on ad spending cut amid longer-than-anticipated geopolitical tension. I assign MNTN a P/S multiple of 1.5x, which aligns with its TTM P/S today.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $9.5 per share, implying around 15% upside. At this point, I give MNTN a buy rating. To account for the risk factor and be a bit conservative in my projection, I give each scenario a 50% probability.

Conclusion

Since IPO, MNTN has successfully delivered decent operating results despite underwhelming share performance. Today, it remains a profitable ad-tech leader focusing on CTV, a segment I believe will continue to grow largely due to secular shifts in TV viewership. I expect decent revenue growth to continue, with the possibility of a surprise if its partnership with another CTV-focused player on the SSP side, MGNI, turns out to be successful. As such, execution remains key despite minimal foreseeable risk today. I believe my projection, while assuming a potential overperformance in the bull scenario, remains conservative since I also assign a 50-50 probability despite minimal risk. My price target analysis suggests that MNTN appears undervalued today.

data.title: A Perspective On Why The Oil Price Is More Likely To Reach $150 Than $50
data.description:
The essence of this is simple enough. Currently, the world is missing somewhere between 10 and 20 million barrels of oil per day. This is equivalent to 10-20% of the global oil supply. Of the global energy base, oil makes up roughly 30%, meaning that 6% of global energy is currently missing. There is no GDP without energy, no industrial activity. The most recent examples of energy shocks of this magnitude are the 1973 and 1979 oil crises, where 6-7% of the global oil supply was disrupted, making this a much larger disruption. We simply have no precedent to compare with.

Why do I say between 10% and 20% when it’s documented that 20% of global oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz? I do so because a few alternatives in the form of inland pipelines exist through Saudi Arabia, but they don’t have sufficient flow rate to compensate for the combined sum of vessels passing through the strait on a daily basis up until the ongoing war started.

“Soon,” I don’t know when, you don’t know when, no one knows when—but “soon” national and local inventories will dry up as the last tankers that went through the strait will, in most instances, have arrived at their destination. At the same time, the global strategic 400 million barrel reserve will provide a buffer, but perhaps not equally. When that happens, the global oil market will have to find a price equilibrium equivalent to the destruction of 10-20% of daily oil consumption.

What will that pricing point be? Again, you don’t know, I don’t know, no one knows, but it will be a lot higher than the price today. Why? Because developed economies will be able to pay a premium in order to prioritize their economic activity, and this will be all about the marginal price at that point. We saw the same thing during the 2022 European gas crisis, where the EU’s ability to pay a premium drove up prices.

In terms of who will be hit the hardest, it’s well documented that the majority of oil and gas passing through the strait reaches Asian economies in particular, but also the European market. The oil market is a global market, so the Americas will also feel the impact, but on a different scale. Having said that, however, stocks won’t differentiate, as this is a global economy, so here, we’re all at risk.

An Abundance Of Dilemmas

If you haven’t already picked up on it, I belong to the nervous part of the crowd observing the current situation unfold. This will show itself in how I view the situation throughout the coming sections, giving reflections on different aspects of the situation.

Some dilemmas fade, and others flare up on a daily basis, but here are some of the ones that I find relevant at the current time.

  • Iran is in no hurry to end the conflict, as they are incentivized to ensure this is as costly as possible to act as a deterrent from ending up in a similar situation 6-12 months down the road. Simply put, they were on the receiving end of a 12-day conflict last summer, and the U.S./Israel started bombing them mid-negotiations in February. They need to ensure this bites back hard on the U.S./Israel to void future attacks.
  • U.S. and Israel goals for ending the conflict may not be aligned, meaning Israel could keep the conflict warm after the U.S. wants to end it. Meaning the strait remains closed.
  • Respective negotiation positions are further apart now than before the disagreement escalated into war, suggesting a limited likelihood that a sudden solution is found.
  • Will the strait automatically reopen if the U.S. declares “mission accomplished” and goes home, or would the first point above incentivize Iran to keep it closed until they achieved favorable terms to ensure the conflict doesn’t reappear 6-12 months from now?
  • The U.S. is potentially about to launch a ground-based invasion with the purpose of controlling hundreds of kilometers of coastal area with the purpose of establishing a buffer zone to reopen the strait. As the U.S. has no interest in a “forever” war, what then happens when they depart the area again?

Background & Context

The conflicts and dilemmas evolve daily, so just a few notes on current status.

Below you can see an illustration of the complete halt of maritime traffic taking place in the strait.

Official data above should be observed with respect to GPS signals being turned off, etc., so should be observed with caution despite showing a directionally very clear picture.

The strait is effectively closed due to the credible threat of Iranian strikes on tankers through primarily boat-based operations, as Iran has limited possibility of missile and drone strikes due to them being launched with fixed GPS target positions against a moving target.

This threat has proven credible, as Iran successfully has managed to attack and damage a number of ships, no less than twenty-four so far into the conflict. In fact, Iran only needs to strike a few with regular occurrence to keep the threat credible, causing insurers to cancel policies, prompting maritime traffic to stand still. No vessel operator wants to run the risk of causing a massive oil spill or losing cargo worth hundreds of millions of dollars.

Pricing Development That Illustrates Current Predicament

Depending on whether you take WTI (CL1:COM) or Brent (CO1:COM), the price is a bit different, but both appear to have stabilized at their current levels. WTI has settled at levels below $100 per barrel, while Brent has settled at levels above $100 per barrel.

These are extremely liquid and highly traded commodities, and the trading exceeds the physical trade underneath. As such, a disconnect can appear at the surface, but if we observe jet fuel, heating oil, and other commodities from the original barrel of oil, we observe much different price movements.

Above you can observe the price movements in jet fuel, which has spiked drastically as it’s a less traded commodity, where we can better observe the physical reality.

Above we see the similar pricing development for heating oil—same point, that physical reality is better depicted. These are the little signs where prices are much higher than what $100 per barrel of oil would indicate.

Coming back to oil, it’s my firm belief, as stated earlier, that at one point, the traded price will have to reflect physical reality. Right now, the market is forecasting an end to the conflict in the short- to mid-term. No prolonged conflict, no upward rip in inflation. I’m of the belief that the market is currently misinterpreting the situation.

Fundamental realities haven’t changed just because President Trump communicated productive conversations with Iran, but expectations do, and therefore, we’re still at $100 per barrel, which helps explain the disconnect. Market perception is that the US will declare mission accomplished as it doesn’t have the staying power, similarly to the tariffs and Greenland situation, and then all this goes away.

During the 1973 oil embargo, WTI increased 300% in price, and yes, it lasted longer than the duration of the current crisis. It’s just to illustrate the point that at one point, the trading reality will have to match the physical reality of being 10-20 million barrels of oil short in the global economy on a daily basis, and I believe the physical tightness shown in these other examples is relevant to consider.

If this goes on for weeks longer, we need physical reality to catch up. We’re already now seeing countries introduce limits related to purchasing, for instance, diesel. Thailand, Malaysia, and Pakistan are countries with very limited oil reserves, causing governments to intervene to try and limit societal activity that consumes oil. Less driving, idle flights, etc. United Airlines has now communicated the need to idle upwards of 5% of their flights to limit the impact of an oil price above $100. If this goes on, we’re only seeing the beginning of initiatives such as these.

There is no short-term fix. You can’t make a jet plane go by electricity, you can’t make your combustion engine car drive on electricity, and so it goes.

Major oil shocks have historically been significant catalysts for global recessions, and I see this no different should it continue for a little while longer. Having to take 10-20 million barrels out of the equation represents a pricing point that can push the global economy into recession. Already now, consumers are starting to feel the economic hardship and pain, which will result in worsening consumer sentiments eroding demand, and so the negative business cycle starts.

I referenced the 2022 European gas crisis at the early onset of this article, and I want to add an additional point to that perspective. If the oil price needs to go much higher due to developed nations and economies being able to absorb higher prices for gas, for instance, it will have a ripple effect in driving up the price of its substitute, coal, which would impact poorer countries negatively. This would put enormous economic strain on economies already struggling.

The knock-on effects are also worth reflecting upon, as more than 500,000 containers enter the region through the strait on a monthly basis, with upwards of 250,000 going the other direction. Supply of consumer staples has effectively halted.

Unwrapping Some Of The Dilemma's & Asymmetry In Military Might

Just to recap the prime dilemmas as I consider

  • Iran is in no hurry to end the conflict.
  • Misalignment between the U.S. / Israel for when conditions are met for ending the conflict.
  • Respective positioning between the parties further apart than before conflict erupted into war.
  • Would the strait even reopen if the aggressors declared mission accomplished and halted offensive operations?
  • With a potential ground-based invasion, what’s the long-term solution without having to concede very unfavorable conditions towards Iran.

The U.S. & Israel have effectively carpet-bombed Iran for the past three-plus weeks, and still, Iran maintains the capability to strike back at its enemies and their infrastructure throughout the region. At a very limited scale, albeit, but it remains able to do so.

With Iranian infrastructure crumbling as every day goes by and being massively overmatched in military capabilities, why do I then insist that Iran has little incentive to seek an end to the conflict?

Iran was attacked last summer during the 12-year war, and now again, with the loss of several high-ranking officials as the consequence, posing a real threat to the regime in power. In order to avoid ending up on the short end of the military stick once again, the regime needs to make this as costly as possible for the U.S. (and the world) to ensure there’ll be no third strike on Iran again, possibly ever. The way to ensure that is to keep the global economy hostage for as long as it’s at all possible for Iran.

The people haven’t revolted despite the intense bombing, and there is little reason to believe the regime is about to lose its grip on the country. As such, it’ll remain in place once this campaign is over, reinforcing the need to ensure the point raised before.

I would expect the Iranian regime won’t accept an end to the conflict before very favorable terms are presented. The alternative is a full-scale invasion with all that it takes, and that’s not feasible with the current mobilization in the area in combination with the political downside at home, where the war is quite unpopular across both sides of the aisle.

Then comes the potential misalignment between U.S. and Israeli military objectives. It’s been hard to fully understand what the U.S. goal has been, as various things have been communicated. However, for Israel, this is a long-standing enemy who has posed threats in the direction of total destruction of Israel, so this is their one chance to go at it. As such, I believe Israel is willing to see this conflict last longer in order to cripple the regime fully. What I’m suggesting here is that even if the U.S. pulled out, Israel might not be ready to stop, then of course, the U.S. might force Israel to do so due to ending munitions supply or otherwise.

The last point I want to touch upon is the negotiation stance. It’s difficult for anybody to fully understand the situation, as the U.S. administration gives daily updates of progress and the conflict being weeks, not months, away from ending. The opposite side of that coin is Iran denying any peace talks. President Trump communicated that Iran had been presented with a 15-point ceasefire plan, to which Iran responded with a list of five conditions that had to be met. This included ensuring the war will not recur, payment of war damages and reparations, as well as Iranian sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz.

Negotiations often start with the two parties being far apart, but as both David and Goliath consider themselves in an advantageous position currently, it could take a long while, if even at all, to reach a mutual understanding. Raising the question of whether the U.S. can bomb Iran into submission before the impact on the global economy gets too large and past the breaking point.

This is a conflict where one party is substantially stronger than the other, which has been the case in most conflicts between the US and other states since the second world war. The difference is that Iran has the possibility of inflicting strong direct and indirect damage on the global economy, despite being much weaker, giving them an edge prior opponents didn’t have.

This is about risk perception, and it doesn’t take more than a dinghy boat with explosives to maintain that threat despite being up against the most powerful military on the globe. It’s not about closing the strait; it’s about presenting and maintaining the threat of being able to inflict damage on traffic passing through.

I think Iran was underestimated and that initiative was lost somewhere during the conflict. Iran, with respect to its limited position of power, took some smart decisions, such as dispersing its military decision-making capability while engaging all neighboring states, which at first didn’t appear smart but ensures it’s easier for them to trade blows of similar magnitude in a tit-for-tat exchange under threat of mutual destruction via attacks on energy infrastructure that takes years to repair and makes up a substantial part of the global oil and gas supply.

Arguments Suggesting I'm Wrong

First of all, the U.S. administration keeps communicating that they intend to stop their campaign within weeks, not months. If that’s the case, balance should be able to return without permanent damage to the global economy to an extent where it results in a recession.

Secondly, other major economies and allies have a strong interest in seeing this come to an end. Japan and South Korea, household U.S. allies, are very reliant on the products flowing through the strait, and I would expect they are already leaning hard on the U.S. to wrap it up and go home. Similarly, while not an ally, China would also have a very strong similar interest, as they have no oil production. It’s well known they carry massive reserves, but that doesn’t mean they won’t try and influence the situation. Pakistan, Thailand, Malaysia, and all other major developing Asian nations will want this to end, as energy makes up a significant part of any developing nation's GDP, indicating vulnerability to what’s ongoing.

High oil prices are bad for consumers, and this administration cares about the price at the pump. While the current situation would have impacted the U.S. economy harder in earlier decades, today it’s mostly a distributional problem, meaning it will impact consumers more than the broad economy, and that is something President Trump supposedly wouldn’t be willing to tolerate for an extended period.

So the list goes; there are, of course, arguments in both camps, but the above-mentioned are some of the more likely as to why the conflict would soon come to an end, meaning this article would age poorly.

Investing In The Current Environment

Rarely has there been a better time to deploy the famous Peter Lynch quote.

“Far more money has been lost by investors preparing for corrections, or trying to anticipate corrections, than has been lost in corrections themselves.”

It’s a dilemma, because if the conflict is resolved tomorrow, likely the market would bounce back. If the conflict persists, this could be a bear market scenario not too far down the road. As always, no one knows.

One thing we do know is that energy and defense stocks have appreciated, with most other sectors going in the opposite direction. I don’t think it’s smart to invest in energy and defense, as they are exposed to downside risk at this point from the strong appreciation. Similarly, with the limited visibility of whether this persists weeks or months, it’s not that appetizing picking up stocks that are down 10% as a result of the market volatility over the past month.

In fact, I’d much rather stay on the sidelines until we have better clarity. I have a balanced portfolio of both high- and low-beta stocks, but if I had a portfolio with a higher beta (more volatile) than the broader market, I’d consider the opportunity for taking the top off, take some profits, and await the outcome.

Conclusion

If you made it this far, it’s fairly obvious that I’m concerned. Seen from my chair, this is at a point where it’s close to being out of control. No party feels forced to pull back, and it’s a difficult military objective to ensure the threat towards the strait is neutralized. The Iranian regime is pushed up into a corner, desperate and with nowhere to go but fight as best it can. There is no easy way to end this conflict and threat currently impacting the global oil and gas market, not to mention we didn’t discuss food supply, fertilizer, etc.

This isn’t a market where I want to add to my portfolio, so I’ll grow my cash base and even consider if there are positions in my portfolio that I find to be overvalued, where I would then reduce a bit, as cash feels like the safer option now, even if it means risking a sudden upwards market tear should President Trump communicate tomorrow that the conflict has ended.

data.title: IGF: A Guide To The iShares Global Infrastructure ETF
data.description:
The iShares Global Infrastructure ETF (IGF) is a passively managed fund that invests in large infrastructure companies around the world. This educational overview covers IGF’s strategy, what’s in the portfolio, historical performance, how it has responded in different markets, and how it compares to other global infrastructure ETFs.

How's IGF Structured?

IGF tracks the S&P Global Infrastructure Index, which includes 75 stocks. Eligibility: trade on a major exchange, float-adjusted market cap of $100+ million, average daily volume of at least $500,000.

The index is split into three clusters: transportation, utilities, and energy infrastructure. Up to 15 slots are reserved for emerging-market stocks to ensure they aren’t crowded out by larger names. Each cluster is assigned a target weight: roughly 40% each for transportation and utilities, 20% for energy. No single stock can exceed 5% of the index. The index is rebalanced quarterly.

IGF launched in December 2007 and has ~$10 billion in net assets. The expense ratio is 0.39%.

What's In The iShares Global Infrastructure ETF?

IGF currently holds 76 positions, which is actually one more than the index’s target. The top ten make up ~38% of assets. NextEra Energy (NEE) is the top holding, followed by Spanish airport operator Aena (ANNSF) and Australia’s Transurban Group (TRAUF).

Holdings across the portfolio include regulated electric utilities, toll road and railroad operators, pipeline companies, and airport concessionaires.

The current allocation across the three clusters is shown above. Again, these weights are set by the index methodology, so they don’t drift much between rebalances. Turnover is 14%.

The U.S. makes up about 40% of assets, with the rest being international. Australia, Spain, and Canada each comprise about 8-9%, followed by Mexico, France, and China.

As of March 2026, the overall P/E was 20x with a P/B of 2.5x.

IGF: Income Profile And Taxes

IGF pays distributions semi-annually, in June and December. The 30-day SEC yield is around 2.7%, with a slightly higher trailing 12-month yield. That may be lower than expected from a utility- or energy-heavy portfolio. That’s because the transportation cluster (airports, toll roads, etc.) includes companies that pay small dividends or none at all.

IGF’s dividends are mostly qualified, taxed at the lower long-term capital gains rates. This works well in a brokerage account. But there’s an additional wrinkle with the international holdings. Foreign governments withhold taxes on dividends paid by companies domiciled in their countries. In a U.S. taxable account, IGF passes through foreign tax credits that investors can claim, partially offsetting that withholding. In a Roth, however, there’s no U.S. tax liability to offset, so the foreign withholding is effectively lost. For a fund where roughly 60% of holdings are outside the U.S., that’s not trivial. A traditional IRA or taxable account preserves that benefit.

How Might IGF Fit In A Portfolio?

If you want similar infrastructure exposure, you could try to build your own basket. Utilities is the easy one—the Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) covers that. But IGF’s transportation cluster (toll road concessionaires, airport operators, etc.) doesn't have a clean domestic ETF equivalent. The energy infrastructure piece is mostly midstream pipeline companies. You could cover this through an MLP-focused fund, but taxes would be treated differently. IGF’s value proposition is bundling all three with the international exposure domestic funds don’t provide.

The fund’s recent beta vs. the S&P 500 was around 0.57. Translation: IGF has moved about half as much as the broader market in either direction. Low correlation can be good for diversifying equity-heavy portfolios or reducing overall volatility.

IGF’s returns also depend on movements of the U.S. dollar against the euro, Australian dollar, Canadian dollar, etc. When USD weakens, foreign holdings are “worth more,” and vice versa. For investors whose portfolios are concentrated in U.S. dollar assets, funds like IGF add another layer of diversification. Of course that can also work against you in periods of dollar strength.

How Has The iShares Global Infrastructure ETF Performed?

IGF’s track record is long but uneven. For the first dozen years or so after its 2007 launch, the fund was roughly flat on a total return basis. Dividends offset price declines but didn’t produce much in the way of cumulative gains.

That changed after the pandemic, though, with IGF making gains in four of the past five calendar years. Annualized total returns have been about 14% over the past three years and 10% over five. Since inception, the annualized figure is 4.5%.

IGF has closely tracked its benchmark, which isn’t surprising for a passively managed index fund with a low expense ratio. It has also slightly outperformed the Morningstar category average on a risk-adjusted basis.

How IGF Might Act In Different Markets?

Since ~40% of IGF is in utilities, interest rates are a big factor. When rates fall, bonds and savings accounts pay less, and investors tend to bid up stocks with steady dividends. 2024-2025 was a great example of how rate-cut expectations can boost the fund. But when rates rise, the reverse likely happens: higher-yielding alternatives compete for the same capital.

Transportation holdings (toll roads, airports, etc.) respond to traffic. These types of companies often have contracts tied to inflation, so rising prices isn’t a bad thing. A lasting slowdown in travel (e.g., due to recession) would pressure this part of the portfolio, though.

Energy infrastructure is a different story. Pipeline operators care more about how much oil and gas flows through their systems than commodity prices. Throughput is stable under long-term contracts.

The most important thing to understand here is that these three clusters don’t respond to the same forces at the same time. Utilities are rate-sensitive, transportation is tied to economic activity, and energy infrastructure follows volume and contracts. The underlying businesses keep collecting tolls, transmitting electricity, and moving fuel regardless of what equity markets are doing. That’s part of why IGF has had relatively shallow drawdowns during broad selloffs. The tradeoff is lagging during momentum-led rallies.

How Does IGF Compare To Peers?

I found three other ETFs with similar global infrastructure exposure. I’ve ordered them from most to least similar.

The State Street SPDR S&P Global Infrastructure ETF (GII) tracks the same index as IGF. Their returns have been nearly identical, with small deviations due to the fee difference (0.40% vs. 0.39%) and how each fund handles dividend reinvestments, cash management, and rebalance execution. The main difference is size. GII has ~$840 million in assets vs. IGF’s ~$10 billion, and its daily trading volume (~50K shares) is a fraction of IGF’s (~975K). That can matter for those who care about liquidity and tight bid-ask spreads.

The ProShares DJ Brookfield Global Infrastructure ETF (TOLZ) tracks a different index which requires companies to earn at least 70% of their cash flows from infrastructure operations. IGF’s index picks stocks based on industry classification; TOLZ’s applies a stricter revenue test. The resulting portfolio leans more heavily into utilities and energy infrastructure—about 77% currently vs. the 62% for IGF. Transportation exposure is much smaller. Speaking of small, TOLZ has just ~$180 million in assets, so liquidity could be a concern.

The FlexShares STOXX Global Broad Infrastructure Index Fund (NFRA) casts a wider net, covering energy, transportation, utilities, communications, and government outsourcing. The latter category includes hospital operators, prisons, and postal services under government contracts. Communications stocks like Verizon (VZ) and Deutsche Telekom (DTEGY) make up nearly a quarter of NFRA’s portfolio, which is quite different from IGF.

Summary

The iShares Global Infrastructure ETF is a passive, index-based fund. It invests in around 75 large infrastructure companies across developed and emerging markets. The portfolio is split roughly 40/40/20 between utilities, transportation, and energy infrastructure. Returns tend to come mostly via regulated cash flows, usage-based revenues, and dividends. That gives the fund a defensive profile.

This article answers four key questions about IGF:

  • What is IGF’s strategy, and what does the portfolio hold?
  • How has IGF performed since its inception?
  • How might IGF respond to different market environments?
  • How does IGF compare with other global infrastructure ETFs?

Author’s Note: This analysis reflects data and information available as of March 2026.

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Ondas: From Drone Niche To Defense Platform (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in November last year, I was bullish on Ondas Inc. (ONDS) after the company reported strong Q3 results. I am now even more bullish after the Q4 earnings release, which I consider to be an inflection point in how the market perceives this company.

The highlight in Q4 was a step-change in FY2026 guidance to at least $375 million. To put that value into perspective, that figure is more than double the company’s prior $170 million to $180 million outlook issued only days earlier and implies roughly 7x growth versus FY2025 revenue of $50.7 million.

While the bears are arguing about the quality of that growth, I am not overly concerned with the recent acquisition spree. Management disclosed that $230 million, or 61% of the FY26 guidance, comes from the recent 5 acquisitions in Q1 2026, leaving roughly $145 million from the pre-Q1 2026 acquisition base. Even though that figure is not pure organic revenue, as it still includes contributions from last year's acquisitions, the organic revenue growth in Q4 was 63% YOY, a decent figure in my view.

That's just the tip of the iceberg of my bull case on Ondas and the reason why I upgraded to a Buy. Below, I explain more.

A Revenue Inflection Point

By far, the main highlight in Q4 earnings was the FY 2026 revenue guidance of at least $375 million.

For reference, just a few days prior to the Q4 release, the company guided $170 million-$180 million in FY 2026 revenue, excluding the impact of any new acquisitions announced in 2026.

In other words, in just a few days, the company more than doubled its FY26 outlook, although bear in mind that the latest guidance includes the list from acquisitions.

When comparing the $375M projected for FY26 with the revenue in 2025 of $50.7 million, the increase is 7x.

Interestingly, the company provided a very detailed breakdown of the contribution to FY26 revenue of each of its 5 recent acquisitions. Specifically, $230 million of the >$375 million guide comes from the Q1 2026 acquisitions.

Notably, the company exited Q4 with only $68.3 million in backlog. In other words, the backlog covers only about 18% of the FY26 guide floor. This means that the company is betting on a massive increase in orders this year.

As you may know, the company has a product offering beyond just drones, so the backlog is exposed to multiple product lines. For reference, the company offers a scaled autonomous defense platform, including counter-UAS, intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR), loitering munition systems, and unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs).

One of the areas that has received a lot of attention recently is the counter-UAS segment. Earlier this month, the company announced approximately $6 million of new orders for dozens of counter-drone systems from current defense and homeland security customers in the Middle East and other regions. As noted during the earnings call, CEO Ryan Hartman noted some preparation work for operations like Epic Fury:

We are also actively preparing for the inclusion of our technology in support of active U.S. Department of War operations like Epic Fury.

Still within the counter-UAS segment, the company announced in February a new multi-million-dollar European order for Iron Drone Raider. Unfortunately, Ondas didn't disclose the exact figure, although the PR noted that it was part of a prior $16.8 million in orders to protect two major European international airports.

Still within Europe, Sentrycs (an Israeli counter-UAS company acquired at the end of last year) disclosed in February a deployment with a German State Police office, with more than 1,000 suspicious drone flights in Germany during 2025. Interestingly, the Sentrycs technology was used within Swisscom Broadcast’s DroneDefence deployment at the World Economic Forum in Davos.

The other product line that I like is intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR).

Earlier this month, the company announced a $10 million investment in World View (a high-altitude balloon ISR and stratospheric remote sensing company). Just a few days later, Palantir, Ondas, and World View announced a strategic partnership to build an AI-enabled multi-domain ISR program (well, it's actually three programs: Warp Speed, AI Flight Director, and SkyWeaver).

From a timing perspective, the PR said that the integration across Ondas’ broader portfolio could begin as early as Q4 2026.

Based on the significant jump in the Street's revenue models below, I can conclude that Q4 earnings were an inflection point in how the market perceives this company.

Risks

Top-line growth is driven by acquisitions, which made me wonder how much organic growth is behind the revenue figures.

The income statement within the latest investor deck shows the exact figure: 63% YOY organic growth in Q4 (vs. 629% YOY including acquisitions).

Undoubtedly, there is a massive gap between organic and inorganic growth. Therefore, I would closely monitor this metric in the next earnings reports.

I provide below my estimate for organic growth this year.

Management guided >$375M in FY26 revenue, including the new acquisitions this year. As I've shown in the last section, $230M of the $375M comes from these recent acquisitions alone. So, the implied 2026 revenue excluding the five 2026 acquisitions is about $145 million, equal to roughly 186% growth versus the total revenue in 2025.

Not bad, although that value downplays the 7x YOY growth after considering the contributions from the 2026 acquisitions.

Looking Ahead

I remain bullish on the company, and I upgrade my rating to a Strong Buy.

Valuation has nothing to do with my upgrade. In fact, if one looks at the 12.6x forward P/S multiple, one may easily conclude this stock is grotesquely overvalued and move on.

My argument is that the guidance reset in Q4 was a true inflection point for the company. In my view, Ondas is now re-rating from a small drone story into what I see as a scaled autonomous defense platform, with offerings like counter-UAS, ISR, and unmanned ground vehicles. That opens the potential for multiple contracts on both sides of the Atlantic.

In the U.S. specifically, the main tailwind that I see is the $1.5T U.S. defense budget for 2027 proposed by Trump earlier this year. For reference, the 2026 budget was $901B, so we're talking about a $500B potential increase in just a year. I strongly believe part of that excess budget will spill over to drone programs, and I see Ondas as a strong beneficiary, particularly in its counter-UAS segment.

In the meantime, profitability is still far away. In the last earnings report, Ondas said Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA losses are expected to increase, while company-wide profitability is still targeted for Q1 2028. In the meantime, I think the cash and cash equivalents position of $1.55 billion is more than enough considering the FY 2025 cash outflow of just $38.75M.

Overall, I see Ondas as a Strong Buy, although I expect the recent stock's volatility to persist until the conflict in Iran comes to an end.

data.title: GUT Is Good, But XLU Is Better

Introduction

At the end of last year, I predicted that 2026 would be an anemic year. So far, my predictions have been too true. I am also anticipating rocky years for 2027-2029. With this in mind, I wanted to identify investments that could help us weather the coming storm. My initial search led me to the Gabelli Utility Trust (GUT), but I also wanted to look at the broader index. After I did that, I believed the State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) was a better choice.

About The Gabelli Utility Trust

Here are some factoids about GUT to help one understand how it operates.

Universe

Formed in July 1999, The Gabelli Utility invests in companies that generate or distribute electricity, gas, water, and telecommunications services, as well as in infrastructure. Its benchmarks are the S&P 500 Utilities Index and the Lipper Utility Fund Average.

Investment Process

GUT is not an index fund. GUT focuses on companies with regulated cash flows and strong balance sheets. The cash flow must be durable. Earnings must be predictable with rate-based growth, and the companies must have durable asset bases and assured return on equity. Relative to their peers, the companies need to have good value. To generate income for investors, GUT prefers companies with secure dividends.

Portfolio Construction

80% of GUT’s assets are invested in utilities, both domestic and foreign. I like the low turnover (3%) and that it is not overly concentrated in the top ten holdings. These are the top holdings:

My Take

GUT is a solid buy, especially for an investor seeking income. The improved income has generated a better total return than one can generate from XLU. I prefer to focus more on four pieces of critical data. XLU has a better Sharpe Ratio (0.49 v. 0.46) than GTU. That means, compared to the risk-free rate, it is safer. It also has a higher probability of generating a positive return (0.54 v. 0.41), so if one wants to avoid overall losses, XLU is more secure. When it comes to beating inflation (0.45 v. 0.43), again, XLU does a better job. When it comes to alpha (3.60% v. 3.94%), XLU does demonstrate a valid methodology, but GUT is even better.

I guess it depends on what one needs. GUT generates better returns with higher risk, while XLU's returns are more reliable. For a balanced investor like me, I will buy XLU.

data.title: Gilead: Why The 'Immune System Company' Pivot Is No Longer A Bargain (Downgrade)
data.description:
Gilead Overview

Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD) has been a beneficiary of a big pharma rally these past few months.

Although I cannot so much speak for the others, I feel confident that Gilead’s stock appreciation has been driven by fundamentals.

When I last visited Gilead in Sept. 2025 (rating upgrade to Buy), I contrasted a seemingly pessimistic valuation (with implied assumptions of -1% CAGR and 32% FCF margins) with its impressively steady multi-billion-dollar HIV franchise (e.g., Yeztugo) and growing presence in oncology (e.g., Trodelvy). I noted that should GILD’s growth assumption bump to merely 4%, its per share value could increase to $162.28.

Its stock came awfully close ($157.29) to $162.28 earlier this year before settling under $140/share.

I see that Gilead has since reported FY25 earnings and has been active in M&A (Ouro Medicines). So, I wanted to take another look to see if it’s still worth buying here.

FY25 Performance

Gilead’s main play continues to be HIV. Due to pricing pressures, the company is tasked with transitioning its portfolio from daily pills (i.e., Descovy) to its long-acting injectables (i.e., Yeztugo).

This isn’t just about money, though. Long-acting injectables are also better for patients. Treatment compliance is paramount in HIV.

So, Gilead’s eggs are still very much in the “HIV basket,” but, as I pointed out last year, Trodelvy continues to shine.

In January, Trodelvy became a preferred (NCCN Category 1) first-line option for mTNBC patients. This was big because, until just recently, Trodelvy was reserved for second-line or later.

Analysts expect Trodelvy to reach $3B in peak annual revenue, so it could support growth for quite a while.

Gilead’s “sore thumb” continues to be its Cell Therapy segment. Its Kite-enabled first-mover advantage is being eroded by newer technologies. Specifically, off-the-shelf bispecifics like AbbVie’s (ABBV) Epkinly and Roche’s (RHHBY) Columvi offer quick turnarounds. Whereas Gilead’s Yescarta requires a patient’s cells to be shipped to a lab, modified, and flown back. This can take up to 17 days, which is a long time when you have an aggressive cancer.

Gilead’s CAR-Ts are also facing competition from other CAR-Ts, like Bristol-Myers Squibb’s Breyanzi. Breyanzi is increasingly favored due its relative safety benefits.

Its solution is to replace its legacy CAR-T products with anito-cel, a next-gen therapy targeting BMCA for multiple myeloma that was acquired for $7.8B from Arcellx. Gilead is also investing in in vivo CAR-T via its earlier $350M acquisition of Interius BioTherapeutics.

Gilead’s most recent acquisition of Ouro Medicines adds OM336, a bispecific antibody (TCE). This one is going after a completely different market than oncology and HIV: autoimmune diseases like Lupus and ITP. By targeting BCMA to deplete B-cells, the hope is to cause a permanent “immune reset” rather than daily immunosuppression.

So Gilead is really going after “big ideas” in the “B-cell depletion” market, which could have multiple applications.

While I like these moves from Gilead (they are effectively trying to become an “Immune System Company”), I don’t think they are coming from a position of strength. Due to limited diversity and ongoing headwinds in Cell Therapy, Gilead has no choice but to make changes.

That said, a giant and stable HIV franchise, combined with a grower in Trodelvy, gives Gilead a solid ground to move on. So that these acquisitions do not appear entirely out of desperation.

Valuation Assumptions

Because GILD is 20% more expensive since my Sept. 2025 analysis, I wanted to revisit its valuation assumptions.

First, I want to get an idea of what Gilead has been doing these past eight quarters.

When it comes to trends, nothing in particular sticks out to me. Gilead’s average FCF margin is 33.3%. Its average revenue growth rate is 2.2%, but has improved in recent periods.

This informs my DCF model assumptions.

Year 1 Revenue of $30B is Gilead’s official guidance for FY26.

I modeled for 3.3% (on average) Revenue Growth with a Moderate Decline Growth Profile (Yrs 1–3: 4.0% → Yrs 4–6: 3.3% → Yrs 7–9: 2.6%). This comes out to a realized CAGR of 3.2%.

FCF Margin of 32% appears a bit conservative compared to Gilead’s last eight quarters, but I suspect that its M&A spree will result in higher R&D investments. So, I wanted to model for some buffer there.

A Beta of 0.8 ("normalized forward") is pretty typical for a large pharma stock, and accounts for pipeline risk and capital intensity.

Most everything else is either standard DCF methodology or was derived from Gilead’s most recent financial statements.

Given that my DCF Value per Share of $138.39 falls within a dime and three pennies within the Current Stock Price, this is my “Base” case for GILD.

Scenario Analysis

But what would a “Bear” or “Bull” case look like?

In the “Bear” case, the realized CAGR drops to 2.4%, which is nearly identical to GILD’s 2% Terminal Growth assumption. Its FCF Margin drops considerably to 28% to reflect increasing pricing pressure and R&D expenditures. GILD’s per share value drops to $107.54.

In the “Bull” case, the realized CAGR jumps to 4.5% with a Peak Mid-Cycle Growth Profile (Yrs 1–3: 3.2% → Yrs 4–6: 6.9% → Yrs 7–9: 3.7%). This will need some clinical trial wins/major drug launches. FCF Margin also inches forward to 34%. And, given key pipeline advancements, GILD’s Terminal Growth assumption jumps to 2.5%.

Reverse DCF Analysis

Going back to my “Base” case, which aligns very closely to implied market assumptions, there is really a combination of variables that can result in the current market price of $138.26.

For example, let’s say GILD’s average Revenue Growth falls to 2.3%. Then it would need 34% FCF Margins to support its current valuation.

It's not detailed in the image, but growth moves GILD's value nearly 2x that of margin improvements. That is, for every 1pp improvement in growth, GILD's valuation jumps ~7%. Whereas it jumps ~3.5% for every 1pp improvement in margin.

Monte Carlo Simulation

Finally, my Monte Carlo simulation displays 10,000 simulations of my “Base” case…to give us an idea of what GILD can trade between before it becomes unreasonably over- or undervalued (assuming no material changes to base assumptions).

I like to compare these results ($98.03 to $209.40/share) to GILD’s 52-week range ($93.37 to $157.29/share).

The model’s 5th and 95th percentile values ($98.03 and $209.40/share) represent a realistic trading range for GILD. GILD could reasonably trade near $98/share without becoming majorly undervalued. It could be near $200/share without being overvalued. Again, this is barring major changes to key assumptions (i.e., realized CAGR of 3.2% → 2% Terminal). In one example, if anito-cel becomes the de facto SoC in MM, GILD’s growth assumptions throughout the projection period would be materially higher, lifting this “range.”

Conclusion

In its current state, GILD appears appropriately valued. The market has picked up on Gilead’s strengthening HIV franchise fortress and Trodelvy’s outperformance since my last look. But there’s a lot of reason for caution, too. Gilead is leaning heavy on M&A expenditure to “stop the bleeding” from their Cell Therapy segment (which was also acquired). Its “B-cell depletion for all” strategy is uncertain and may not ever pay off. And, after all, despite the appeal of its generous 2.4% dividend yield, even with the stock’s recent rally, GILD is up merely 44% in the past ten years!

So, I’m happy to lock in these gains and downgrade GILD to Hold pending major clinical developments.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - Middle East Uncertainty To Dominate Ahead Of Jobs Report, Nasdaq 100 At 6-Month Lows
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Another week dominated by the happenings in the Middle East. The uncertainty around whether or not a deal would materialize between the US, Israel and Iran kept markets on edge and hanging on the words of President Trump.

Markets hovered between brief bouts of risk-on sentiment, but the risk-off environment dominated the majority of the time. As I write this, the US is waiting for troops to land in the Middle East, with about 3,000 troops from the Army’s 82nd Airborne Division expected to land Friday.

This was followed by a Wall Street Journal report later in the day that the Pentagon is ​looking at sending ‌up to 10,000 additional ​ground troops ​to the Middle East ⁠to ​give President Donald ​Trump more military options. This comes as President Trump ​weighs peace talks with ‌Tehran, ​citing ⁠Department of Defense ​officials with ​knowledge ⁠of the planning.

Heading into the weekend, the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq retreated to six-month lows on Friday, as a sell-off in technology stocks weighed heavily on the broader market. While the Dow Jones Industrial Average remained relatively flat for the period, both the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq are now pacing toward their fifth consecutive week of losses.

This downturn highlights a period of extreme uncertainty, evidenced by the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) climbing 1.57 points to 29.01, a clear sign that investor anxiety is reaching a fever pitch.

The Nasdaq’s recent performance officially confirms a move into correction territory, ending Thursday more than 10% below its record close. It follows in the footsteps of the Russell 2000, which was the first major index to signal a correction last week.

Meanwhile, the commodities complex has faced its own share of volatility. After hitting a four-month low of $4,097.99 on Monday, spot gold staged a recovery to trade above $5,300/oz for much of the week. Despite this bounce, the precious metal remains under significant pressure as markets continue to price in a more hawkish outlook for interest rates.

Silver and PGMs (platinum group metals) saw a reprieve on Friday, with spot silver gaining 4.4% to reach $71.01 per ounce, while platinum and palladium rose by 3% and 3.7%, respectively.

In the energy sector, oil prices edged higher on Friday, though Brent crude is still eyeing its first weekly decline since early February.

This comes amid shifting geopolitical headlines. President Trump indicated that talks with Iran were progressing, though he offered little in the way of specifics. The underlying supply crunch remains severe, however, as the conflict has sidelined roughly 11 million barrels per day. The International Energy Agency has characterized the current situation as a supply crisis more severe than the combined impact of the dual oil shocks of the 1970s.

How did FX markets perform?

The US Dollar is eyeing its strongest monthly gain since July 2025, fueled by safe-haven demand and rising expectations for a domestic rate hike. The Dollar Index (DXY) rose to 99.973 on Friday, up 2.4% for March.

This strength has pushed the yen toward the critical 160.00 level, a threshold widely seen as a trigger for potential BoJ intervention. Despite rising Japanese bond yields and hawkish signals from policymakers, the yen remains pressured by Japan’s high sensitivity to energy import costs.

Elsewhere, the euro held steady at $1.1529, while sterling marked its fourth straight decline to $1.3311.

Risk-sensitive currencies also struggled; the Australian dollar touched a two-month low of $0.6887, having shed 3% since the start of the conflict. This leaves the Aussie as the second-worst major performer this month, trailing only the Indian rupee, which has dropped nearly 4%.

Markets are currently focused on whether central banks will hike interest rates in April following energy price spikes caused by conflict in the Middle East. While markets are pricing in a high probability of hikes, I think this may be premature.

The Week Ahead

Here is the summary of key events for next week in the US, Eurozone (EU), and UK:

United States

Retail Sales (Wednesday): Expected to be lifted by strong auto sales, but there is concern that rising energy costs are becoming "demand-destructive," leaving consumers with less money for discretionary spending.

Non-Farm Payrolls / Jobs Report (Friday): This is the marquee event. While a rebound to around +60,000 jobs is expected (following a weak February impacted by strikes and weather), the underlying trend is described as "low-hire, low-fire," with most sectors outside of government and healthcare actually losing workers over the past year.

ISM Manufacturing Index: Likely to show strength as customers rush orders to get ahead of potential price hikes in manufactured goods.

Eurozone (EU)

Economic Sentiment (Monday): Sentiment is expected to drop significantly. Consumer confidence has already "taken a nosedive" due to the dual fears of geopolitical turmoil and higher prices.

Inflation Data (Tuesday): Preliminary March readings are expected to show a sharp increase in headline inflation driven by surging petrol and diesel prices at the pump.

Manufacturing vs. Services: Analysts will be looking at the European Commission's data to see how energy-intensive industries are holding up compared to the services sector, which has recently seen a drop in activity.

United Kingdom (UK)

BoE Decision Maker Panel (DMP) Survey: This survey of CFOs will be closely watched for wage expectations. While wage growth has been falling, "hawks" at the Bank of England are concerned that expectations remain higher than desired.

Inflation Expectations: There is specific concern regarding consumer perceptions of inflation, which have spiked alongside petrol prices. This is noted as one of the few data points that might "panic" the Bank of England into a rate hike.

Rate Hike Speculation: Despite market pricing of three hikes this year, ING notes reports that BoE Governor Bailey was "infuriated" by the hawkish repricing, suggesting the bank is further from hiking than investors believe.

China

PMI Data (Tuesday/Wednesday): The focus is on whether manufacturing returns to expansionary territory.

Official NBS PMI (Tuesday): Expected to rise to 50.0 (up from 49.0 in February). This would be a significant milestone, as the index has been in contraction for 10 of the last 11 months.

Caixin (RatingDog) Manufacturing PMI (Wednesday): Expected to continue its trend of outperforming the official NBS indicator.

Japan

Tokyo CPI (Friday): Markets will closely watch inflation data for the capital. Both headline and core inflation are expected to remain stable in March, as rising gasoline prices are likely offset by government utility subsidies and steady food costs.

Economic Activity Data: February figures for industrial production and retail sales are expected to decline, partially reversing the strong gains seen in January.

Chart of the Week - US Dollar Index (DXY)

The US Dollar Index (DXY) is currently testing a critical juncture as it battles a confluence of technical resistance levels.

After a sharp recovery from the January lows, price action has carved out an ascending channel, but the bullish momentum is now stalling near the 100.617 handle.

While the index remains above its key SMAs (20, 50, and 200), a failure to break and hold above the psychological 100.00 level could trigger a retracement toward the 98.72 support zone.

The RSI is currently hovering around 61, indicating that while bulls maintain control, the window for a sustained breakout is narrowing.

Traders should watch for a decisive daily close above the channel for continuation or a breakdown below 99.57 to confirm a short-term reversal.

US Dollar Index (DXY) Chart, March 27, 2026
