data.title: eGain: Needs To Maintain The Momentum To Attract Me
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to revisit eGain Corporation (EGAN) now that it has released full-year 2025 results recently and go through the performance over the year and what is still in store for it.

Last time I covered EGAN, it was still too expensive, but since then, it has declined around 22% and has become a lot more attractive, especially if it can keep the momentum.

Briefly on Performance

So, now that the company has released its full-year 2025 results, let’s take a look at how it performed. Starting from the top, we can see that throughout the year, EGAN’s revenue numbers continued to climb; however, the growth wasn’t anything to write home about. Over the last 4 quarters, the company’s top-line growth averaged under 5%. In the latest earnings report, we can see that revenues came in at 2.6% growth compared to last year, ending with around $23m in revenues. The company’s star service, the AI Knowledge Hub, boasted a 27% ARR growth to $48.4m. It now represents around two-thirds of the company’s total revenue generated. Professional services continue to dwindle and will most likely disappear over the next few years. It came in at $1.2m vs. $1.5m a year ago. Hardly a drag anymore on the company’s top-line performance.

With the increase in high-value product mix, let’s take a look at how the company’s margins developed throughout the year. Unsurprisingly, these have improved as well, and quite decently.

Margins across the board expanded tremendously, with the biggest differences coming from EBIT and EBITDA margin performance. These improvements can be attributed to the increase in the AI Knowledge Hub becoming the biggest revenue generator, which was a very successful product. I didn’t expect to see AI improving the knowledge hub this much, but it is what it is. Ignore the inflated net margin, as that is not what it is right now. It is inflated because of a tax benefit it received in FY 2025.

Let’s look at the company’s financial health. EGAN finished the year with around $83m in cash and equivalents, against no debt, apart from some operating lease liabilities. So, in short, it is a great financial position to be in. Another impressive thing is the company’s ability to generate cash. It improved tremendously over the same time last year, going from $7.4m to $20.5m. A whopping 178% increase. Free cash flow is around $20m, as the company has an asset-light model, making it easy to scale without increasing capex significantly.

In my opinion, the company has progressed quite well over the last year. The top-line growth could be a bit more impressive, but the product mix, with the AI taking over almost completely, delivered substantial increases in profitability, which I value a lot more than the headline performance numbers. If the management can maintain this momentum, I can see EGAN’s value increasing in the future even more than it has in the past.

AI Knowledge Hub - core growth strategy

It is the engine of growth of today, and I’m all for it. It is not often you see such an improvement in a company’s profitability in such a short time. This is the key to higher-quality recurring revenue going forward, and I am glad the company has pivoted in such a way that should be beneficial in the long run. There is also a recurring theme I have noticed with these types of companies, and that is that most of the positives come from enterprises. Enterprises are the ones with deep pockets, so it makes sense to put a bit more focus on them, especially when the product seems to be doing a lot more for them than AI solutions for consumers.

As I mentioned earlier, AI Knowledge Hub grew its ARR by 27% y/y and was 64% of total SaaS ARR. It seems that investors care about this product because it is not just a feature but is right now at the center of EGAN’s monetization model. The demand for it is there, so it is only a matter of whether the management is not going to drop the ball and lose the momentum it gathered this year.

AI SaaS companies have been hit quite a bit over the last while due to the whole “SaaSpocalypse” fiasco, where investors feared that the traditional SaaS companies are in a dire situation since AI agents can replace their services quite efficiently. When I saw the decrease in the share price of EGAN, I was actually surprised to see that it wasn’t hit as hard as many others. This essentially told me that the company may be a lot less exposed than the traditional SaaS companies. The reason for this is that its AI Knowledge Hub is much closer to a vertical AI infrastructure than it is to another workflow app, which the business world is full of. AI cannot bypass EGAN’s UI and do the work that needs to be done because AI still needs trusted content, access controls, and workflow integration to produce reliable answers.

EGAN has that knowledge, that trusted knowledge-system integration. This integration is built for specific business problems, not for broad, generic ones, so an AI plug-in is not going to suffice because the AI knowledge hub makes sure the answers are approved, compliant, traceable, and consistent across the many agents, chatbots, and self-service agents. So, the company has something really good on its hands.

What does it mean for margins

That is what I would like to tackle next. We already know that the product is successful and a lot more resilient than a generic workflow tool, which means it offers really good value. What kind of margin improvements in the long run should we expect if this momentum is sustained just a bit longer? Certainly, EGAN’s gross margins are already trading like it is a premium SaaS company, but its other margins are just beginning to look attractive.

Over the next half-decade, I don’t expect the company to reach software-elite margins of something like 30%+ EBITDA. The company is just too small for those kinds of numbers because to reach those, a lot of investments to scale will be necessary, and that is going to affect its profitability. Now, if the company can consistently improve the product, upsell it, and gain more customers along the way, I can see the momentum in margins sustained, and over the next five or so years, reaching mid-teens or low 20s even. Adjusted EBITDA was already at 14% in the latest quarter, so I can see the low 20s as very much achievable. Let’s take a look at some valuation assumptions and projections.

Valuation

Below are the assumptions I used for my model.

Revenue growth needs to improve before I consider changing it from such low numbers. As I said earlier, I can see the company improving its margins close to the low 20s, but I decided to go just a bit under for conservatism. I would like to see how much of an improvement it can make over the next couple of quarters and whether I need to change my low 100bps annual improvement. The rest I kept close to their historical averages. Additionally, I went with the 8% as my discount rate instead of the lower 6.5%, which, according to the internet search, is the current WACC of the company. I wanted to add a little cushion to my assumptions.

These assumptions give me the following EPS and revenue projections.

And with these projections, we get the following intrinsic value for EGAN.

Closing Comments

So, I think it will all depend on the management’s ability to properly maintain and increase monetization of its premier offering, the AI knowledge hub, so that it doesn’t fall behind the competitors. I think it has the ability to do just that; however, because I need some proof that it can grow its profitability at a much higher pace, I am maintaining a hold rating for now. I will be tuning in for the next couple of quarters to see the progress, as its share price is getting close to my entry price.

data.title: Tiptree: Annoying Sell-Off But Value Unaffected
data.description:
It has been a while since I wrote about Tiptree Inc. (TIPT). There hasn't been much to say. We are just waiting for the sale of Fortegra to close. That is expected in the late April to late May/early June timeframe. I don't believe there will be an antitrust issue. It's just a matter of regulatory approval, which I believe should be granted without any issues.

Operations of Fortegra do not matter right now. All profits are trapped at the entity. They will be the buyer's unless the deal falls through. Should the deal not close before June, the purchase price increases at a 10% annual rate. Again, I don't expect that.

The sale of Reliance has closed. After the Fortegra sale closes, management said in the Q4 earnings announcement that it will have a $24.40/share pile of cash. With the stock at $16.70, that is a 31.5% discount to cash value that should be attained in about 2 months at the latest.

I know that some people are disappointed with the Fortegra sale price. Frankly, I am, too. My previous estimates of value clearly assumed higher multiples of earnings. However, I have no doubts that management got the best price available. Management has discussed their concerns about a softening insurance underwriting environment. Companies like Kinsale (KNSL) and Skyward (SKWD) have reflected that softening. I believe that is why the sales price underwhelmed some people.

No matter the price, the fact of the matter is that the deal is likely to close, and there will be cash of $24.40 on the balance sheet with no debt or other liabilities. As important, the company has cut a lot of overhead, enough that with the cash in T-Bills, the interest will more than cover corporate overhead.

I am also of the belief that if the stock is at a level where management can buy it at close to a 1/3 discount from the cash value, that will represent a 50% return on any shares it buys, and it will do so aggressively at that level. In addition, every share it buys at such a discount increases cash/share.

The other upside of having a big mound of cash (almost $1 billion) in a declining operating environment is that assets come down in price. I believe that Michael Barnes would like to buy back into the insurance business and mix that with the asset management group he already has in place (the old Tiptree Capital). If the insurance downcycle lasts long enough, I believe there will be assets to be had for cheap.

Should Barnes and crew find an attractive acquisition target this time round, I think he'd like to fix some problems the old Tiptree had: specifically, overhead inefficiency. I was never shy about criticizing the overhead at Tiptree. It was always too high. Given the structure of Tiptree/Fortegra, the company had dual roles for many corporate functions: two CFOs, two COOs, a CEO at Fortegra, and Co-CEOs at Tiptree in addition to a President. The list went on. WR Berkeley (WRB) and RLI Corp (RLI) run much more efficiently at the corporate level. I believe Michael Barnes would like to replicate that model the next go-round.

If I believe the overhead cuts will represent the new overhead model for Tiptree going forward. That means more profits of any purchased businesses going to shareholders rather than overhead.

Risk:

Once the Fortegra deal closes, the main risk here is that Tiptree management overpays for a business. Management's track record is generally very good. Fortegra was a home run deal. However, management has made missteps: mostly notably the sale of the real estate for a large block of Invesque. That investment cost shareholders over $100 million and management countless hours of distraction. Every investor makes mistakes. I believe Barnes and crew will be extremely judicious with whatever purchase they make. The market is already discounting anything they buy by over 30%.

Conclusion:

Once the Fortegra sale closes, I think shares have little downside from the current price levels ($16.70). Much below that, I believe management will aggressively buy back shares. Michael Barnes has established a 10b5-1 plan to purchase 2 million shares (he doesn't have to wait for cash to come in the door). Clearly, he sees value. This management team has delivered value to shareholders. I think the market is overdiscounting their ability to do so again.
